questions.question: What are the four pillars of OOP?
questions.explanation: Object-Oriented Programming rests on four pillars: 1. Encapsulation — bundling data and methods, hiding internal state. 2. Abstraction — exposing only essential features, hiding implementation. 3. Inheritance — a subclass acquires properties and behaviours of a superclass, promoting code reuse. 4. Polymorphism — one interface, many forms. Achieved via method overloading (compile-time) and method overriding (runtime).

questions.question: What is the this keyword? What is the super keyword?
questions.explanation: this refers to the current instance of the class. Used to differentiate instance variables from local variables, to call another constructor of the same class (constructor chaining), and to pass the current object as an argument. super refers to the immediate parent class. Used to call the parent class constructor, access parent class methods/fields that are hidden by the child.

questions.question: What is a constructor? What are its types?
questions.explanation: A constructor is a special method used to initialize objects. It has the same name as the class, no return type, and is called automatically when an object is created. Types: - Default constructor — no arguments, provided by Java if none is defined. - Parameterized constructor — takes arguments to initialize fields with custom values. - Copy constructor — takes another object of the same class and copies its state (not built-in in Java, but commonly written manually).
questions.note: Constructors are NOT inherited. If a parent has only a parameterized constructor, the child must call super(args) explicitly.

questions.question: Explain the static keyword in Java.
questions.explanation: static means the member belongs to the class, not to any instance. All instances share the same static member. - Static variable — shared across all objects (e.g. a counter). - Static method — can be called without creating an object; cannot access non-static members directly. - Static block — runs once when the class is loaded, used for static initialization. - Static inner class — nested class that does not need an outer class instance.
questions.note: static methods cannot use 'this' or call instance methods directly.

questions.question: What is the final keyword in Java?
questions.explanation: The final keyword restricts modification: - final variable — value cannot be changed after assignment (constant). - final method — cannot be overridden by subclasses. - final class — cannot be extended (e.g. String, Integer are final).
questions.note: A final reference variable cannot point to a new object, but the object's internal state can still be changed.

questions.question: What is encapsulation? What is abstraction? How are they different?
questions.explanation: Encapsulation is bundling data (fields) and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (class), and restricting direct access to some components using access modifiers. It is about hiding data. Abstraction is hiding the implementation details and showing only the essential features to the user. It is about hiding complexity. Key difference: Encapsulation wraps data and code together; Abstraction hides the internal workings from the outside world.

questions.question: What is the Diamond Problem? How does Java handle it with interfaces having default methods?
questions.explanation: The diamond problem occurs when a class inherits from two sources that both define the same method. Java resolved multiple inheritance of classes but introduced it again with Java 8 default methods in interfaces.
questions.note: ✦ Rule: If two interfaces provide default implementations for the same method, the implementing class must override it to resolve ambiguity.

questions.question: Is Java pass-by-value or pass-by-reference? Explain with examples.
questions.explanation: Java is always pass-by-value. For primitives, the value itself is copied. For objects, the reference (memory address) is copied — not the object.

questions.question: What is the difference between Shallow Copy and Deep Copy?
questions.explanation: Shallow copy copies field values — but object references point to the same nested objects. Deep copy duplicates every nested object too.

questions.question: What are Wrapper Classes? Explain Autoboxing and Unboxing.
questions.explanation: Wrapper classes wrap primitive types as objects, enabling use in collections and providing utility methods.
questions.warn: ⚠ Unboxing a null wrapper throws NullPointerException.

questions.question: What is the difference between Class, Abstract Class, and Interface?
What is the difference between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer?
What is method overloading vs method overriding?
Explain the concept of Packages in Java and why they are used.
questions.explanation: Packages group related classes/interfaces for organization, modularity, and avoiding name clashes.
questions.note: ✦ Three purposes: ① Code organization ② Maintainability/modularity ③ Avoid name clashing

questions.question: What is the Java Collections Framework? Explain List, Set, and Map.
questions.explanation: The Java Collections Framework provides a unified architecture of interfaces and classes to store and manipulate groups of objects. List — ordered, allows duplicates. Main implementations: ArrayList (fast random access), LinkedList (fast insert/delete). Set — unordered, no duplicates. Main implementations: HashSet (O(1) ops), TreeSet (sorted), LinkedHashSet (insertion order). Map — key-value pairs, keys unique. Main implementations: HashMap, TreeMap (sorted keys), LinkedHashMap.

questions.question: What is the difference between ArrayList and Vector?
What are Generics? Why are they needed? What are the restrictions?
questions.explanation: Generics provide type safety at compile time, eliminating ClassCastException at runtime and removing the need for casts.
questions.note: ✦ Generic type erasure: at runtime, all type parameters become Object. Generic info only exists at compile time.

questions.question: What is the difference between HashMap, LinkedHashMap, and TreeMap?
questions.code:
Map<String, Integer> hash   = new HashMap<>();     // no order guarantee
Map<String, Integer> linked = new LinkedHashMap<>(); // insertion order
Map<String, Integer> tree   = new TreeMap<>();      // sorted by key

What is the volatile keyword? When is it used?
questions.explanation: volatile guarantees visibility — changes made by one thread to a volatile variable are immediately visible to all other threads (bypasses CPU cache/local memory).
questions.note: ✦ volatile solves visibility but NOT atomicity. Use AtomicInteger/synchronized for compound operations.

questions.question: What are default and static methods in interfaces (Java 8)?
What is multithreading? What is the difference between process and thread?
questions.explanation: Multithreading is the concurrent execution of two or more threads within a single process to perform multiple tasks simultaneously, improving CPU utilisation and application performance. Process — an independent program with its own memory space and resources. Thread — the smallest unit of execution within a process, sharing the same memory space with other threads of the same process.

questions.question: What is thread safety? What is a daemon thread?
questions.explanation: Thread safety ensures that shared code or objects can be accessed by multiple threads simultaneously without causing data corruption or unexpected behaviour. Achieved via synchronized blocks, volatile, Atomic classes, or concurrent collections. Daemon thread is a background thread that provides services to user threads (e.g. garbage collector). It is automatically terminated by the JVM when all user threads finish — it does not prevent JVM shutdown.
questions.note: setDaemon(true) must be called before start(). Calling it after throws IllegalThreadStateException.

questions.question: How do you create threads in Java? What is the difference between Thread and Runnable?
questions.tip: ✅ Prefer Runnable/ExecutorService over extending Thread — keeps class hierarchy flexible.

questions.question: What is synchronized? Explain race condition and deadlock.
What is the difference between wait(), sleep(), and yield()?
What is Callable vs Runnable? What is Future?
What are the different types of SQL commands? (DDL, DML, DCL, TCL)
questions.explanation: SQL commands are grouped into four categories: DDL — Data Definition Language: defines/modifies structure. Commands: CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE, RENAME. DML — Data Manipulation Language: manipulates data. Commands: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE. DCL — Data Control Language: controls access. Commands: GRANT, REVOKE. TCL — Transaction Control Language: manages transactions. Commands: COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT.

questions.question: What is normalization? Explain 1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF.
questions.explanation: Normalization is the process of organizing database tables to reduce data redundancy and eliminate insertion, update, and deletion anomalies by dividing larger tables into smaller, related ones. 1NF (First Normal Form): Each column contains atomic (indivisible) values; no repeating groups. 2NF (Second Normal Form): In 1NF + every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on the entire primary key (no partial dependency). 3NF (Third Normal Form): In 2NF + no transitive dependency (non-prime attribute depending on another non-prime attribute). BCNF (Boyce-Codd NF): Stricter 3NF — for every functional dependency X→Y, X must be a super key.
tableRows: Every determinant is a super key

questions.question: What are SQL Joins? Explain INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL OUTER, and SELF JOIN.
questions.explanation: A JOIN combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column. INNER JOIN: returns only rows with matching values in both tables. LEFT JOIN: all rows from left table + matched rows from right (NULL for no match). RIGHT JOIN: all rows from right table + matched from left (NULL for no match). FULL OUTER JOIN: all rows from both tables (NULL where no match on either side). SELF JOIN: joins a table with itself — useful for hierarchical data.
questions.code:
-- INNER JOIN
SELECT e.name, d.department_name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id;

-- LEFT JOIN (all employees, even without a dept)
SELECT e.name, d.department_name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id;

-- SELF JOIN (employee and their manager)
SELECT e1.name AS employee, e2.name AS manager
FROM employees e1
LEFT JOIN employees e2 ON e1.manager_id = e2.id;

-- Flip 0↔1 in a column
UPDATE t1 SET col1 = CASE WHEN col1 = 1 THEN 0 ELSE 1 END;

questions.question: What is the difference between clustered and non-clustered indexes?
questions.explanation: An index is a data structure that speeds up data retrieval. Clustered index: physically reorders the table data to match the index. Only ONE per table. The primary key is a clustered index by default. Leaf nodes contain actual data rows. Non-clustered index: a separate structure with pointers to the actual data. Multiple allowed per table. Leaf nodes store index key + pointer to data row, not the data itself.

questions.question: What is SQL Injection? How do you prevent it?
questions.explanation: SQL Injection occurs when user input is directly concatenated into SQL strings, allowing attackers to alter the query logic.
questions.warn: ⚠ Never use string concatenation with user input in SQL queries!

questions.explanation: Dependency Injection (DI) means an object's dependencies are provided externally rather than the object creating them itself. This removes coupling and improves testability.

questions.question: What is AOP? Explain its terminologies.
questions.explanation: Aspect-Oriented Programming separates cross-cutting concerns (logging, security, transactions) from business logic.

How do you deploy a Spring Boot application on AWS EC2?
What is the Spring Bean PostProcessor? How is it different from init()?
Explain Hibernate Entity lifecycle states.
What is the difference between get(), load(), save(), persist(), merge(), and update()?
questions.code:
// FETCH methods
em.find(User.class, 1);   // get() → hits DB, returns null if not found
session.get(User.class, 1); // same as find()
session.load(User.class,1); // returns PROXY (lazy), throws if not found on access

// SAVE methods
session.save(user);    // Hibernate-only, returns PK, INSERT immediately
em.persist(user);     // JPA standard, INSERT on commit

// UPDATE methods
session.update(user); // re-attach detached, assumes ID exists
em.merge(user);       // JPA standard: SELECT then INSERT/UPDATE, returns new instance
session.saveOrUpdate(user); // save if new, update if exists

// REMOVE methods
em.remove(user);       // JPA standard DELETE
session.delete(user);  // Hibernate-only

// SESSION CACHE methods
session.evict(user);   // detach one entity
em.detach(user);       // JPA standard detach
session.clear();       // detach ALL entities
session.refresh(user); // re-fetch from DB, overwrites in-memory state

questions.question: What is Spring Data JPA? How does it work?
What is the Hibernate load() method? How does it use proxies?
What is MVC Architecture?
questions.explanation: MVC (Model-View-Controller) is an architectural pattern that separates an application into three layers: Model — handles data and business logic; interacts with the database. View — UI layer; renders data received from the Controller. Controller — handles incoming requests, instructs the Model, selects the View. Benefits: separation of concerns, easier testing, parallel development, reusability.

questions.question: What is statelessness in REST? What are the RESTful design principles?
questions.explanation: Statelessness: each request from client to server must contain ALL information needed to process it. The server stores no client session state between requests. This makes the system scalable, reliable, and easy to maintain. RESTful design principles (6 constraints): 1. Uniform Interface — consistent resource naming + HTTP methods. 2. Client-Server — separation of UI and data storage concerns. 3. Statelessness — no session on server side. 4. Cacheable — responses can be cached for better performance. 5. Layered System — intermediaries (load balancers, gateways) can exist transparently. 6. Code on Demand (optional) — server can send executable code to client.
questions.note: Idempotent methods: GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS. Non-idempotent: POST (each call may create a new resource).

questions.question: What is the difference between REST and SOAP? How do you choose?
questions.explanation: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style using HTTP, JSON/XML, lightweight, stateless. Preferred for public APIs and web services. SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) is a protocol with strict contracts (WSDL), XML-only, supports WS-Security. Preferred for enterprise/banking systems needing high security and formal contracts.

questions.question: Explain Spring MVC request processing flow.
What is REST? Explain HTTP methods used in REST APIs.
questions.note: ✦ REST is lighter than SOAP — no WSDL, no envelope, just HTTP + JSON. Any language/platform can consume it.

questions.question: What is CORS? When and how do you configure it?
questions.explanation: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is needed when a browser app makes HTTP requests to a different origin (different domain/port/protocol) than the one that served the web page.

questions.question: What is JWT? Why is it used in REST APIs? What is its structure?
questions.explanation: JWT (JSON Web Token) is a compact, URL-safe token for stateless authentication. REST is stateless so each request must carry proof of identity.
questions.note: ✦ JWT can be READ by client (base64 encoded) but NOT MODIFIED (signature verification fails).
